# 5-Step Solution to 99% of Cannabis Growing Problems
by Nebula Haze
* * *
**Table of Contents**
[Step 1: Perfect Your Watering Game](https://www.growweedeasy.com/5-step-solution-to-most-cannabis-growing-problems#watering-roots) (become a master of root health)
[Step 2: Decipher Mystery Nutrient Problems & Deficiencies](https://www.growweedeasy.com/5-step-solution-to-most-cannabis-growing-problems#nutrient-deficiency)
[Step 3: Craft the Perfect Environment to Stop Common Problems](https://www.growweedeasy.com/5-step-solution-to-most-cannabis-growing-problems#environment)
[Step 4: Shine a Light on Cannabis Grow Light Issues](https://www.growweedeasy.com/5-step-solution-to-most-cannabis-growing-problems#grow-lights)
[Step 5: Uncover the Invisible Enemy: Is it Actually a Pest?](https://www.growweedeasy.com/5-step-solution-to-most-cannabis-growing-problems#bugs-pests) (sometimes you never see the bugs)
[🔍 Still Unsure? Dive into Our Visual Guide to Cannabis Plant Problems](https://www.growweedeasy.com/5-step-solution-to-most-cannabis-growing-problems#picture-guide-cannabis-grow-problems)
* * *
Before we get into the 5-step cannabis plant solver tutorial, here are some bonus visual resources to help you quickly identify your problem by examining the symptoms.
Check out this [Cannabis Problem-Solving Flowchart](https://www.growweedeasy.com/wp-content/uploads/2014/09/cannabis-plant-problem-flowchart.jpg).
[](https://www.growweedeasy.com/wp-content/uploads/2014/09/cannabis-plant-problem-flowchart.jpg)
Also check out our [cannabis symptom check chart](https://www.growweedeasy.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/11/beta-diagnose-chart-cannabis-problems-symptoms.jpg) (beta version), or continue reading the 5-step cannabis solution article below.
[](https://www.growweedeasy.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/11/beta-diagnose-chart-cannabis-problems-symptoms.jpg)
When it comes to growing cannabis, it’s inevitable that you will eventually run into growing problems. I’ve never met a single grower who’s never had ANY problems.
So don’t beat yourself up when growing problems happen. They happen to every grower!
Overwatering is often difficult to diagnose because it can cause a [surprising number of symptoms](https://www.growweedeasy.com/5-surprising-signs-overwatering-cannabis).
[](https://www.growweedeasy.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/brown-scorch-marks-calcium-deficiency-caused-by-overwatering.jpg)
Our interactive [Plant Doctor tool](https://www.growweedeasy.com/cannabis-symptoms-pictures) shows you how to [identify common cannabis growing problems on sight](https://www.growweedeasy.com/cannabis-symptoms-pictures), but since many problems look similar, you may need a more in-depth diagnosis to pinpoint the root of the issue. This article will walk you through the process of diagnosing almost any growing problem.
Just remember; how you **react** to problems determines whether you harvest tons of potent buds… or end up totally killing your plants. The way you react to problems is a strong reflection of your ability as a grower. Anyone who stays calm and is willing to search for a solution (instead of giving up) is on their way to becoming a true master grower.
Growing cannabis can be easy when you have the right information, and this article will tell you exactly how to react to problems as they arise.
[Nutrient burn](https://www.growweedeasy.com/cannabis-plant-problems/nutrient-burn) is a common grow issue, but some symptoms [look similar](https://www.growweedeasy.com/is-this-marijuana-nutrient-burn) yet are caused by something else. This tutorial will help you determine the true source of the brown tips.
[](https://www.growweedeasy.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/01/nute-burn-brown-tips-leaves.jpg)
Luckily, bringing your plant back to peak health is actually pretty simple… which explains why I can do it.
Arm yourself with the knowledge of these 5 simple things to check when your plants get sick, and you’ll know how to give your plants a 100% recovery.
**These tips were specially made to fix a whole host of possible cannabis growing issues with minimal work.**
Catching and solving problems quickly means **bigger** , **bulkier** , more **potent** buds. Your cannabis plants will love you for it.
* * *
##  Step 1: Perfect Your Watering Game (become a master of root health)
**Summary: Verify Watering Habits & Root Health**
In a metaphorical sense, the roots are the heart of your cannabis plants. When roots become sick, the health of the entire plant quickly falls apart.

Drooping or wilting is the main symptom of watering or root problems, and overwatering is common. Plants droop when they have “wet feet” for too long, usually from being watered too often or too much at a time. Cannabis plants also droop if they get too dry between waterings, and also if their roots get sick.
[](https://www.growweedeasy.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/08/seedling-droopy-coco-not-ready-to-water-yet.jpg)
Yet sometimes, cannabis plants with unhappy roots show no signs that there are problems until things become grim. Luckily, it’s easy to check for issues and even easier to solve them once you know what to do.
Watering and root problems can cause a variety of other symptoms including yellowing, nutrient deficiencies, leaf spots, brown edges, curling, and more.
[](https://www.growweedeasy.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/cannabis-over-watering-root-problems-drooping.jpg)
_What to do:_ Use the checklists below to diagnose whether your plant is over-watered, under-watered, or has a root problem.
**Check for signs of overwatering**
Overwatering is easily the most common grow problem, but it can be tricky to diagnose. Go through this list carefully and if you suspect overwatering, check the link below to see pictures and get detailed solutions.
  * Plants seem droopy (at least some of the time), especially the day after getting watered.
  * Plants seem to be getting nutrient deficiencies even though you’re on top of [nutrients](https://www.growweedeasy.com/nutrients) and [pH](https://www.growweedeasy.com/ph).
  * Brown spots appear on the serrated edges of leaves (not just the tips).
  * Burn or brown markings appear on leaves after watering.
  * Unexplained yellowing or bleaching
  * Slow growth or stunting
  * Curled or stunted leaves
  * Green algae growing on your topsoil
  * Plant feels very heavy for its size (a sign that there’s a lot of water being held in the grow medium)
  * Grow medium seems overly saturated and never dries out on top.
  * If water is not draining freely, or if it takes plants a long time to dry between waterings, you may actually have a problem with drainage.
  * [Little seedling in a big pot](https://www.growweedeasy.com/how-to-water-a-seedling-in-a-big-pot) (it takes care to avoid overwatering in this situation)
  * [Fungus gnats](https://www.growweedeasy.com/cannabis-plant-problems/fungus-gnats) (little black flies) are buzzing around your soil. These only appear when the topsoil stays too wet for too long. If you see fungus gnats, you know for sure that you’re overwatering.
  * Plants are allowed to sit in runoff water after being watered.
  * Smell the root area of your plants. Go on, no one’s looking! If you can, smell the bottom of the pot where the excess water comes out. The water that runs out the bottom should generally have a neutral smell. A ‘stinky’ smell is an indicator that your plant may be suffering from ‘root rot’ or other problems due to high levels of moisture for too long.

[Learn how to spot and treat overwatering](https://www.growweedeasy.com/5-surprising-signs-overwatering-cannabis) (with tons of pictures and a comprehensive watering guide)
[](https://www.growweedeasy.com/wp-content/uploads/2010/10/cannabis-seedling-that-has-been-over-watered.jpg)
**Check for signs of under-watering**
  * Plants seem droopy (at least some of the time) or wilted
  * Plants seem better right after getting watered
  * Nutrient deficiencies appear even though you’re on top of nutrients and pH
  * Dark green coloring
  * Slow growth
  * Grow medium looks dry or dusty
  * Grow medium is very airy and drains quickly
  * Plant feels very light for its size (this means there’s not much water in the grow medium)

[Learn how to spot and treat under-watering](https://www.growweedeasy.com/cannabis-plant-problems/underwatering) (not as common, but it happens!)
[](https://www.growweedeasy.com/wp-content/uploads/2013/03/under-watered-cannabis.jpg)
**Root Rot**
  * Roots can get mushy and “rot” in a grow medium that stays really wet for a long time, though typically root rot happens in a hydroponic setup
  * The main symptoms of root rot are when roots appear brown, slimy, or twisted.
  * Roots may smell musty like something has “gone bad”.
  * Plants display symptoms that are similar to over and under-watering (deficiencies, discolored leaves, drooping, etc.)
  * [Learn how to prevent and treat root rot](https://www.growweedeasy.com/cannabis-plant-problems/marijuana-root-rot)

In hydroponics, root health is key to success. React quickly if you notice plants showing signs of droopiness.
[](https://www.growweedeasy.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/02/curling-leaves-from-cannabis-root-rot.jpg)That cannabis plant is drooping because its roots are affected by root rot. In this picture, you can see the unhealthy parts of the roots turned brown.
[](https://www.growweedeasy.com/wp-content/uploads/2014/07/2013-07-17-root-rot.jpg)
**Tips for healthy roots**
  * **Start with a great grow medium** including [super soil](https://www.growweedeasy.com/super-soil-how-to-grow-organic-weed-easy), [good potting soil](https://www.growweedeasy.com/what-soil-is-good-for-growing-cannabis), [coco](https://www.growweedeasy.com/coco-coir), or [hydro](https://www.growweedeasy.com/high-yield-bubbleponics-technique). Don’t use dirt you find outside or low-quality potting mixes like Miracle-Gro soil.
  * **Water potted plants properly!** Make sure water can drain freely from potted plants, and don’t water too often. [Learn how to water potted cannabis plants ](https://www.growweedeasy.com/how-often-water-marijuana)so you never run into problems.
  * **Make sure that roots get plenty of oxygen**. With potted plants, the growing medium should be relatively loose as opposed to dense. Roots need oxygen to stay healthy. If you have a thick grow medium, mixing in some [perlite](https://www.amazon.com/Espoma-PR8-8-Quart-Organic-Perlite/dp/B002Y0AK6S/ref=as_li_ss_tl?dchild=1&keywords=perlite&qid=1592699991&sr=8-2&linkCode=ll1&tag=4200a7-20&linkId=81776a7ef63c5f3b640b7366d158e776&language=en_US) is an easy way to add more pockets of air. In a hydroponic setup where the roots are growing directly in water, the more bubbles the better (up to a point), as this dissolves lots of oxygen directly in the water. Water that is at a lower temperature can also hold more oxygen than water at a higher temperature. That means controlling the temperature is extra important in a hydroponics setup.
  * **If you’re using a non-standard growing medium** (anything besides soil, coco coir, hydro, etc.), know how to take care of your plants in their growing medium. For example, it can be difficult to grow cannabis in something like straight perlite unless you maintain a water reservoir because perlite can not hold any water. Straight rockwool is bad for the opposite reason, it holds onto a lot of water and not enough air.
  * **Your plant roots cannot be allowed to dry out or sit in stagnant water**. Roots need a good mix of both moisture and oxygen for the fastest growth
  * **Add a root supplement** to your water to encourage healthy roots.

[Hydroguard](https://www.amazon.com/Botanicare-HYDROGUARD-Bacillus-Inoculant-1-Quart/dp/B00IGFH25M?ie=UTF8&qid=1525715681&sr=8-3&keywords=hydroguard&th=1&linkCode=ll1&tag=4200a7-20&linkId=0ea8abd399716ae44c8f18654a2b9f9f&language=en_US&ref_=as_li_ss_tl) is my favorite root supplement, it’s cheap and will build up colonies of good bacteria that out-compete the bad bacteria and actually promote plant growth. Awesome for [hydroponic tubs](https://www.growweedeasy.com/high-yield-bubbleponics-technique), but helpful in any situation where you’re trying to inoculate roots and keep them healthy.
[](https://www.amazon.com/Botanicare-HYDROGUARD-Bacillus-Inoculant-1-Quart/dp/B00IGFH25M?ie=UTF8&qid=1525715681&sr=8-3&keywords=hydroguard&th=1&linkCode=ll1&tag=4200a7-20&linkId=0ea8abd399716ae44c8f18654a2b9f9f&language=en_US&ref_=as_li_ss_tl)
Another common root supplement is [kelp extract](https://www.amazon.com/Neptunes-Harvest-SW118-Liquid-Seaweed/dp/B009HQRHS6?dchild=1&keywords=kelp%2Bextract%2Bneptune&qid=1592701411&sr=8-2&th=1&linkCode=ll1&tag=4200a7-20&linkId=dc61e96f753f3769f46bd1146818f496&language=en_US&ref_=as_li_ss_tl), usually provided as a small amount of liquid fertilizer that contains seaweed. Seedlings love it. Kelp supplements can help protect against heat stress and root problems with potted plants but are usually not suitable for hydroponic setups as the organic material can clog up the tubing.
[](https://www.amazon.com/Neptunes-Harvest-SW118-Liquid-Seaweed/dp/B009HQRHS6?dchild=1&keywords=kelp%2Bextract%2Bneptune&qid=1592701411&sr=8-2&th=1&linkCode=ll1&tag=4200a7-20&linkId=dc61e96f753f3769f46bd1146818f496&language=en_US&ref_=as_li_ss_tl)
##  Step 2: Diagnose Nutrient Problems & Deficiencies
Cannabis nutrient problems are often closely related to the pH at the roots of your plants. That’s because when the root pH is off, it can cause your plant to show signs of a nutrient deficiency, even if the nutrients are actually there in the soil.
Even if you’re providing nutrients, plants may get nutrient deficiencies if the pH is off. This [magnesium deficiency](https://www.growweedeasy.com/cannabis-plant-problems/magnesium-deficiency) is the result of the pH being too low at the roots.
[](https://www.growweedeasy.com/wp-content/uploads/2010/06/cannabis-magnesium-deficiency-yellow-leaves.jpg)
Growers need to [monitor their root pH](https://www.growweedeasy.com/ph) in almost all growing setups for the best results. Cannabis plants prefer a slightly acidic root environment. Some growers get lucky and the pH of their water happens to be just right without additional adjustment or monitoring. Yet most growers will achieve better growth and plant health by monitoring pH. Being aware of your pH helps you understand when things are going wrong. Luckily, nutrient and root pH problems are easy to solve with the right information.
Cannabis plants cannot absorb nutrients properly if the pH at their roots is too high or too low. So even if the nutrients are present, you’ll still get what look like nutrient deficiencies if you don’t maintain the right pH. This [copper deficiency](https://www.growweedeasy.com/cannabis-plant-problems/copper-deficiency) is the result of bad pH even though the plant was getting nutrients in the water.
[](https://www.growweedeasy.com/wp-content/uploads/2013/12/copper-deficiency-yellow-tips-leaves.jpg)
For those of us who aren’t lucky enough to magically have the right pH, here’s how to ensure your plants get exactly what they need.
_What to Do:_ The goal is to give your plant exactly the kind of nutrients it needs to thrive and provide the correct pH at the roots so your plants can properly absorb nutrients.
First, if you are providing your plants with nutrients, [check your nutrients](https://www.growweedeasy.com/nutrients) to make sure they have all the necessary elements and minerals for proper growth. If you’re not sure what they need…
Soil: An easy and simple nutrient system for beginning cannabis growers in soil is**[Fox Farm Liquid Nutrient Soil Trio](https://www.amazon.com/Fox-Farm-Liquid-Nutrient-Formula/dp/B00D93NIFY?keywords=fox+farm+trio&qid=1707287564&sr=8-3&linkCode=ll1&tag=4200a7-20&linkId=f94b631d3a94547a79dfab73074ecddf&language=en_US&ref_=as_li_ss_tl)**.
[](https://www.amazon.com/Fox-Farm-Liquid-Nutrient-Formula/dp/B00D93NIFY?keywords=fox+farm+trio&qid=1707287564&sr=8-3&linkCode=ll1&tag=4200a7-20&linkId=f94b631d3a94547a79dfab73074ecddf&language=en_US&ref_=as_li_ss_tl)
Soil, Coco, or Hydro: A time-tested, simple nutrient system for growing cannabis in soil or hydroponically (including in soilless mediums like coco coir) is the **[General Hydroponics Flora Trio](https://www.amazon.com/Hydroponics-GH-Flora-FloraMicro-FloraBloom/dp/B01HG98GX0?dchild=1&keywords=flora+trio&qid=1631401043&sr=8-5&linkCode=ll1&tag=4200a7-20&linkId=1f30e1339fd0f2bc662e5b631a985533&language=en_US&ref_=as_li_ss_tl).**
[](https://www.amazon.com/Hydroponics-GH-Flora-FloraMicro-FloraBloom/dp/B01HG98GX0?dchild=1&keywords=flora+trio&qid=1631401043&sr=8-5&linkCode=ll1&tag=4200a7-20&linkId=b5fbb480e6da6ea852f8030664f5a77c&language=en_US&ref_=as_li_ss_tl)
If you want to cut through the technical marketing talk and get straight to what works, it’s important you learn a little bit about what kind of nutrients are needed by the cannabis plant during different stages of growth. These days, there are lots of nutrient systems available that are specifically formulated for plants like cannabis, and they help take all the guesswork out and give your plants exactly the nutrients they need. It doesn’t have to be expensive to provide everything your cannabis plant needs. [Learn everything you need to know about cannabis-friendly nutrients](https://www.growweedeasy.com/nutrients).
Second, even if you are providing your plants with the correct amount of nutrients, you still need to monitor and adjust the pH to prevent nutrient deficiencies. [How to check & adjust pH for growing cannabis](https://www.growweedeasy.com/ph).
[](https://www.growweedeasy.com/ph)
When you’re sure you’ve got the watering, nutrients, and pH balance right, issues with your cannabis plant should begin to improve fast. Just keep in mind, cannabis leaves that were harmed from nutrient issues probably won’t turn green again. The**real sign you’re on the right track** is when the **problem stops getting worse** and the n**ew leaves grow in green and healthy**.
##  Step 3: Craft the Perfect Environment
If you’ve ruled out problems with watering, nutrients, and pH, there’s a strong chance something with the environment is causing your cannabis symptoms.
A good growing environment is crucial to the health of your plants. When the [temperature](https://www.growweedeasy.com/temperature-growing-cannabis) (and to a lesser extent the [humidity](https://www.growweedeasy.com/humidity-growing-cannabis)) is too high or too low, your plants will not grow properly no matter what else you do. Plants in a sub-optimal environment often appear unhealthy and buds just don’t fatten up. You may also end up with problems related to plant stress such as [hermies](https://www.growweedeasy.com/male-plants-bananas-hermies).
[](https://www.growweedeasy.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/12/vegetative-vs-flowering-temperature-v3.jpg)
Hot plants tend to be weak and easily stressed. On top of that, when your plant gets too hot it will drink more water than usual. This is because the plant is trying to keep itself cool and hydrated by moving more water through its system and letting it evaporate from its leaves (a process known as **transpiration**). But drinking more water can lead to other problems too. For example, in hot, dry conditions, your plant might end up with [nutrient burn](https://www.growweedeasy.com/nutrient-burn-cannabis), which is when the tips of the plant curl or turn brown because the plant is taking in more nutrients along with the extra water.
If there is a particular source of heat that is harming the plant (like a hot grow light), the leaves nearest to that light will start to **wilt** and turn a shade of **brown**. [Learn more about how cannabis plants respond to heat stress](https://www.growweedeasy.com/heat-light-stress).
If the whole growing environment is too hot, the symptoms start to show up all over the plant. You might even notice your plant drooping like it’s thirsty or overwatered, even though you’re sure you’ve watered it just right.
If a plant is exposed to extreme heat for an extended period, it might not recover. In severe cases, the plant can remain permanently wilted and stop growing altogether.
[Heat stress](https://www.growweedeasy.com/cannabis-plant-problems/heat-light-stress) dramatically slows down cannabis growth and buds don’t fatten as much as they could.
[](https://www.growweedeasy.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/07/heat-wave-light-stress-brown-curling-leaves.jpg)
One of the reasons you need great air circulation is your plants “breathe” CO2 during the light hours, almost like we breathe oxygen. Unless your plants are in a big open space with lots of air, or you are supplying your plants with CO2, you need to have some sort of [exhaust system](https://www.growweedeasy.com/hps-grow-lights-setup#how-to-set-up-exhaust) to pull away hot, stale air and bring in new fresh air for your plants.
If you’re worried about the smell of cannabis, make sure any heated air being vented outside grow room/house **gets carbon filtered** to remove the scent of cannabis. For example, many growers choose to get a carbon scrubber to “scrub” all odors before the hot air is vented. By creating [good suction with an efficient exhaust system](https://www.growweedeasy.com/hps-grow-lights-setup#how-to-set-up-exhaust) and adding a carbon scrubber, you can basically eliminate all smells from the grow room, inside and outside of the house. [Learn more about controlling odors from the grow room](https://www.growweedeasy.com/how-to-control-smell-when-growing).
If you’re concerned about the smell of cannabis, you can use a carbon filter to clean the hot air before it leaves your grow room or house, which removes the cannabis scent. A carbon filter (also sometimes called a “carbon scrubber” because it scrubs odors out of the air) is perfect for this task and cleans all the odors before the hot air is let out. By using a [good exhaust system](https://www.growweedeasy.com/hps-grow-lights-setup#how-to-set-up-exhaust) that creates strong suction and includes a carbon filter, you can virtually get rid of all smells from the grow room, whether inside or outside your house. To learn more, [check out our guide on managing odors from the grow room](https://www.growweedeasy.com/how-to-control-smell-when-growing).
An exhaust system doesn’t have to be anything fancy. At its simplest, an exhaust system can be an intake and exit hole with a fan pointed out from your grow area in the upper hole. Having a fan constantly pulling hot air out and bringing fresh air in from your intake hole will help vent out excess heat and ensure that your plants have the fresh air they need.

Because your plants need CO2 when the lights are on in order to function properly, a _completely sealed-off grow box_ is not good unless you’re artificially adding CO2. Your plants will suffer if they don’t get enough CO2 in a sealed grow box even if the temps are completely under control.
Some growers like to [supplement their plant with extra CO2](https://www.growweedeasy.com/co2-marijuana-yields) in an attempt to increase yields or growth rate, but it’s important to note that the amount of CO2 found in regular air is plenty for your plants unless you have extremely powerful grow lights (1000W HPS, high power LEDs, etc).
For most growers, a proper exhaust system and a few strategically placed fans are all you need when it comes to air circulation.
[Learn how to make an exhaust system for your plants](https://www.growweedeasy.com/exhaust). (it doesn’t have to be hard or cost an arm and a leg)
[Learn more about controlling temperature in the grow room](https://www.growweedeasy.com/temperature-growing-cannabis).
**Watch Your Humidity, too!** Very high or low humidity can cause slow growth, though high humidity is much worse because it can also trigger [white powdery mold](https://www.growweedeasy.com/cannabis-plant-problems/white-powdery-mildew) and [bud rot](https://www.growweedeasy.com/cannabis-plant-problems/bud-rot).
Humidity is a term used to describe the amount of water vapor present in the air at any given place, and the amount of water in the air is related to the local temperature. [Learn more about how temperature and humidity are related](https://www.growweedeasy.com/temperature-growing-cannabis#temperature-humdity-vpd).
Humidity measures the amount of water in the air. Humidity between 40-60% RH works great in most situations when growing weed.
[](https://www.growweedeasy.com/wp-content/uploads/2013/11/humidity-monitor.jpg)
**It is common for new cannabis growers to not pay attention to humidity** at all, and this can sometimes cause otherwise unexpected and/or inexplicable problems.
Cannabis plants can show false signs of nutrient deficiencies when the relative humidity of the grow area drops below 35% RH, especially when things get _really dry_. Younger plants are most susceptible, though plants often adjust over time many plants are able to grow just fine as long as they’re provided with plenty of water at the roots. Some cannabis strains can handle low humidity better than others.
Extremely low humidity can cause plants to grow slower and be less healthy, but typically low humidity isn’t a problem for most growers unless they live in a desert.
[](https://www.growweedeasy.com/wp-content/uploads/2013/04/drying-out-curling-drooping-wilting.jpg)
While established plants are pretty hardy, young plants, new clones, and vegetating plants that are used to humid conditions can be hard hit by a dry spell.
[](https://www.growweedeasy.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/05/cannabis-humidity-chart.jpg)
**In the cannabis flowering stage, humidity should be kept below 55%** to prevent the chance of mold. Low humidity also increases resin/trichome production and helps buds grow more “tight” and dense.
Younger cannabis plants usually are fine with high humidity, but humidity is especially dangerous in the flowering stage because the insides of the buds can grow mold and still look fine from the outside at first.
High humidity in the flowering stage causes [bud rot](https://www.growweedeasy.com/cannabis-plant-problems/bud-rot), which quickly spreads from plant to plant and can ruin an entire harvest overnight. The leaves on this bud became discolored overnight.
[](https://www.growweedeasy.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/02/bud-rot-cola-the-day-after.jpg)
When you look closer, you can see the entire inside has started molding and rotting 🙁 This entire bud had to be tossed, and the rest of the plant was harvested immediately ato prevent the bud rot from spreading to more buds.
[](https://www.growweedeasy.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/02/bud-rot-cola-the-day-after-closeup.jpg)
In addition to preventing bud rot, there’s some evidence that maintaining low humidity environment in the flowering stage helps buds produce more [terpenes](https://www.growweedeasy.com/terpenes-terpenoids-cannabis) (giving buds a stronger natural scent) and more [trichomes](https://www.growweedeasy.com/make-buds-sparkle-with-more-trichomes) (possibly even increasing the potency of your buds!).
_What to Do:_ Get a **temperature and humidity monitor**. Control heat by whatever means necessary and keep humidity in check using the steps outlined below.
Monitor the temperature and humidity with a [thermometer/hygrometer](https://www.amazon.com/Weewooday-Thermometer-Hygrometer-Terrarium-Temperature/dp/B08RS947DG?pd_rd_w=Ow4c6&content-id=amzn1.sym.db26482d-f2e6-4f69-a20a-6ff6cd9fa602&pf_rd_p=db26482d-f2e6-4f69-a20a-6ff6cd9fa602&pf_rd_r=WX18XKAH0M24AYAX6112&pd_rd_wg=Owuip&pd_rd_r=ec4b71a7-b74f-412b-85d0-6e80b9dccb17&pd_rd_i=B08RS947DG&th=1&linkCode=ll1&tag=4200a7-20&linkId=7671ac85f9374d49a062975ee89ccc52&language=en_US&ref_=as_li_ss_tl) in your grow space. I like models with a probe, and multiple thermometers make it easy to measure the temperature in different parts of the grow space (helpful for identifying hot spots from the grow).
[](https://www.amazon.com/Weewooday-Thermometer-Hygrometer-Terrarium-Temperature/dp/B08RS947DG?pd_rd_w=Ow4c6&content-id=amzn1.sym.db26482d-f2e6-4f69-a20a-6ff6cd9fa602&pf_rd_p=db26482d-f2e6-4f69-a20a-6ff6cd9fa602&pf_rd_r=WX18XKAH0M24AYAX6112&pd_rd_wg=Owuip&pd_rd_r=ec4b71a7-b74f-412b-85d0-6e80b9dccb17&pd_rd_i=B08RS947DG&th=1&linkCode=ll1&tag=4200a7-20&linkId=7671ac85f9374d49a062975ee89ccc52&language=en_US&ref_=as_li_ss_tl)
No matter how your growing space is arranged, it’s crucial to have air flowing around your cannabis plants. This helps avoid hot spots, strengthens the plant stems, discourages pests, lowers the risk of mold, and ensures your plants get a good supply of fresh CO2 for optimal growth.
**With fans,** you shouldn’t aim them straight at your plants because it can cause [wind burn](https://www.growweedeasy.com/cannabis-plant-problems/wind-burn). It’s usually better to direct the fans towards a wall to spread the air around, or have the fans blowing above and below the plant leaves. Fans help move air around the room and give your plants a gentle breeze. You can get a [small fan](https://www.amazon.com/Genesis-6-Inch-Convertible-Table-Top-Speeds/dp/B012BKZC86/ref=as_li_ss_tl?dchild=1&keywords=small+fan&qid=1592709076&sr=8-4&linkCode=ll1&tag=4200a7-20&linkId=c269beddff9d9c5d8aff4321b1dd8fbb&language=en_US) for less than $15.

Ideally, you want to arrange your fan system so that all the plant leaves are softly quivering, but none of the stems or branches are being pushed around by the wind’s force. If you see stems waving around, it’s too much wind.
**When the humidity is too low** , you need to make sure your plants always have enough fresh water. You might have to cut back on the amount of nutrients because the plants will drink more water, which means they’ll also take in more nutrients from their roots compared to when the air isn’t as dry.
When it comes to controlling _humidity_ indoors, you may need to use a **humidifier** and/or a **dehumidifier** to keep the humidity in the optimal range. A humidifier adds moisture to the air while a dehumidifier takes moisture out.
Controlling your grow room humidity is one of the tricks that separates expert growers from beginners. Proper humidity levels can increase bud quality, density, sparkle, smell, and even potency.
[](https://www.growweedeasy.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/11/grow-tent-with-315-lec-grow-light.jpg)
You can get some pretty fancy [humidity gauges](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07Z6F5S2M/ref=as_li_ss_tl?psc=1&pd_rd_i=B07Z6F5S2M&pd_rd_w=aBduB&pf_rd_p=a64be657-55f3-4b6a-91aa-17a31a8febb4&pd_rd_wg=LRiX8&pf_rd_r=TA5M7941MA3WSDR50KAR&pd_rd_r=27743b0e-aa7d-4a10-9b93-8edc1c89a893&spLa=ZW5jcnlwdGVkUXVhbGlmaWVyPUFBTkxHTzkwRDlRTzYmZW5jcnlwdGVkSWQ9QTAzNTYzMjgzU1BVVjE2TllRVEZGJmVuY3J5cHRlZEFkSWQ9QTA5OTY1MTExQUU0VUpES0RFU1Amd2lkZ2V0TmFtZT1zcF9kZXRhaWxfdGhlbWF0aWMmYWN0aW9uPWNsaWNrUmVkaXJlY3QmZG9Ob3RMb2dDbGljaz10cnVl&linkCode=ll1&tag=4200a7-20&linkId=2f9c9cac4c4e689616bc1bab26fa0eee&language=en_US) to place in your grow room, and products to help change the humidity are commonly found at department stores, hardware stores, or most stores that sell home appliances. There are also some DIY options discussed in our complete article about [controlling indoor humidity](https://www.growweedeasy.com/humidity-growing-cannabis).
Here are the most important things to keep in mind when it comes to the humidity of your cannabis grow room.
  * **Vegetative stage** : try to aim for a humidity of 40-60% during the vegetative stage
  * **Flowering stage:** keep humidity below 55% (around 45-50% is ideal) during the flowering stage to prevent bud rot, help buds grow more [dense](https://www.growweedeasy.com/why-cant-i-grow-dense-buds), increase resin production, and cause your plant to produce more [terpenes](https://www.growweedeasy.com/terpenes-terpenoids-cannabis) (the stuff that makes your buds smell great). However, lowering the humidity below 35% RH can increase the chance of your plant running into problems or deficiencies, especially with a plant that’s used to higher humidity.

With the right tools, the task of maintaining humidity can be close to automated, and you will see benefits compared to the amount of time and money you invest.
When the air feels moist or humid (or a humidity monitor alerts you to high humidity), it’s extra important to ensure that plants get plenty of [air circulation](https://www.growweedeasy.com/5-step-solution-to-most-cannabis-growing-problems#step4-air-circulation) and have lots of air movement. You also want to avoid letting leaves touch each other during periods of high humidity. You can fix constantly too-humid conditions with an exhaust system, fans, and possibly a dehumidifier.
If plants are very leafy and leaves are lying on top of each other, it dramatically increases the humidity of the grow space. In this case, removing leaves ([defoliation](https://www.growweedeasy.com/nebulas-3-step-defoliation-tutorial-2020)) can help. Try to remove leaves that are in the lower/middle part of the plant, leaves that are covering bud sites, and leaves that are touching each other making wet spots.
[](https://www.growweedeasy.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/03/white-powdery-mold-strikes-big-leafy-plants.jpg)
When the air is too dry indoors, you can either choose to let your plants grow hardened to the arid air, or you can use something like a “swamp cooler” ([evaporative cooler](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B093L3H152?pd_rd_i=B093L3H152&pd_rd_w=YFCVw&pf_rd_p=887084a2-5c34-4113-a4f8-b7947847c308&pd_rd_wg=PA6Mb&pf_rd_r=NTFC47RWV4QHYK4XM286&pd_rd_r=ee79e650-ed36-4210-8ac5-5dce093166b7&spLa=ZW5jcnlwdGVkUXVhbGlmaWVyPUFJWklGUkU3UUlFUlYmZW5jcnlwdGVkSWQ9QTA0NDY5MzIxVDRKM1BVUldDVUU2JmVuY3J5cHRlZEFkSWQ9QTAzNjI0NjIxWTQ2OEZGM0ZXMFA4JndpZGdldE5hbWU9c3BfZGV0YWlsJmFjdGlvbj1jbGlja1JlZGlyZWN0JmRvTm90TG9nQ2xpY2s9dHJ1ZQ&th=1&linkCode=ll1&tag=4200a7-20&linkId=89da94961c2b5d23fe0014f9d8c839f1&language=en_US&ref_=as_li_ss_tl)) to add humidity while also reducing the temperature of your room. A humidifier will also add humidity but tends to raise the temperature of the room, which is usually not ideal.
[Learn everything you need to know about controlling humidity in the grow room](https://www.growweedeasy.com/humidity-growing-cannabis).
##  Step 4: Identify Cannabis Grow Light Issues
Cannabis loves light! But not too much…
When growing cannabis, the mantra when it comes to lights is usually “More is better!” To be honest…it’s true! More light usually is better!
[](https://www.growweedeasy.com/wp-content/uploads/2014/09/marijuana-more-light-equals-more-buds.jpg)
You see, light is like “food” for your marijuana plants. When plants are growing very slowly, but aren’t showing signs of other problems, the cause is often that they aren’t getting enough light.
The amount of light you give your plants is strongly correlated with how fast your plants grow, as well as how much buds your plants will produce during the flowering stage.
[Learn 15 tactics to increase cannabis yields](https://www.growweedeasy.com/15-ways-to-increase-cannabis-yields-indoors).
Many new growers don’t realize they’re not giving their plants enough light. As a very general rule, you want _at least_ 100W worth of electricity going into your indoor grow light to harvest a few ounces. More is often better.
**More light = More yields + Faster Growth** (up to a point)
If plants are growing very slowly, if they’re not drinking much water, or otherwise aren’t doing well without showing any other major signs of problems, often the problem is the plant simply needs more light to thrive.
As a _very_ general and unscientific rule, a new grower who doesn’t run into major problems can expect…
  * **Fluorescent lights like CFLs or T5s** – Average harvest of 0.25-0.5g/watt
  * **MH/HPS, CMH, and LED grow lights** – Average harvest of 0.5-1g/watt (some [new LEDs](https://www.growweedeasy.com/5-best-led-grow-lights-2025-cannabis-yields-speed-bud-quality#hlg-blackbird-led-grow-light-for-cannabis) can harvest over 1 g/watt)
  * An average grower should expect a harvest on the lower side, an experienced grower can expect a harvest on the higher end (or more!)

This rule refers to how many watts of electricity are actually being p _ulled out of the wall_ , not some sort of “equivalent” amount of watts. If an LED pulls 90W of electricity from the wall, you should plug that into the equation, not the claim that it’s “equivalent” to a 400W HPS light. Same for CFLs, if you have a CFL bulb that pulls 26W of electricity from the wall, you should count that as 26W, and ignore the fact that it says “equivalent to 100W” on the front.
Of course, this rule varies quite a bit depending on the type of light, the strain being grown, the size of the cannabis plants, etc. However, it’s a good general guide if you’re not sure how much light you need, and it can help you decide if you might not have enough light for your plants.
_To put that into perspective…_
  * A good 300W LED grow light will net the **average grower** about 150g or yield, or 5 ounces of dried bud
  * A 300W LED grow light will net the **experienced grower** about 300g, or 10 ounces of dried bud

Keep in mind that there are many other factors that go into this, and this is not a guarantee as much as a general rule. Moreover, many smaller lights tend to get better yields per watt than fewer larger lights. Lights that are kept closer to plants (without signs of light stress) will also tend to produce bigger yields and faster growth. Most importantly, the strain you start with and the [training methods](https://www.growweedeasy.com/how-to-grow-short-bushy-marijuana-plants) you use to get your plants to more efficiently use your indoor grow lights will have a _huge_ impact on your growth rates and final yields.
**But can there be _too much_ light?**
[Yes, there can be too much light!](https://www.growweedeasy.com/too-much-light)
Unfortunately, more light is not _always_ better. Cannabis plants can be fickle. You want to give them very bright light… but not _too_ much light.
When marijuana plants are young, they are weak and cannot tolerate much light at all. Yet as they get older, and especially in the flowering stage, marijuana plants want almost all the light they can get.

The problem of **too much light** often occurs **indoors** , when your grow lights are too close to the cannabis plants. This essentially gives them their own version of **sunburn**.
Young plants, seedlings, and new clones are especially prone to suffering from too much light, though it can affect older plants too. The main way you know your plants are suffering from light burn is if only the leaves closest to the lights are affected, and you’ve already covered all the other possible issues in this article.
The intensity of a grow light that is **too close** will dry up a plant, cause bleaching/yellowing, turn the edges of its leaves brown and crispy, almost like nutrient burn, and other odd symptoms. [Learn more about light-stressed plants](https://www.growweedeasy.com/cannabis-light-burn).
Too high light levels will cause [light burn](https://www.growweedeasy.com/cannabis-plant-problems/light-burn).
[](https://www.growweedeasy.com/wp-content/uploads/2015/11/led-light-burn-top-leaves-yellow.jpg)
High Pressure Sodium (HPS) lights have the meanest reputation for ‘light-stressing’ plants, but many of the newer, more powerful LEDs (Light Emitting Diodes) do it, too.
[Learn more about all the available cannabis grow lights](https://www.growweedeasy.com/growing-marijuana-what-type-of-lights)
_What to Do:_ Most importantly, pay attention to your plant and watch out for signs of light stress, which will only affect the parts of the plant closest to your grow lights.
For**HPS, Metal Halide,** and **Fluorescent** grow lights (all the grow lights that use bulbs instead of LED diodes), use the ‘back of your hand’ test.
**Back of Your Hand Test** – For MH, HPS, CFLs, and T5 grow lights (not always effective for LEDs as they put off a lot of light even when they’re not putting off a lot of heat)
  1. Set up your grow lights and turn them on (make sure they’ve had time to warm up)
  2. Gently place your hand – palm down – right above the tallest part of your plant
  3. Wait 10-15 seconds
  4. If the heat coming from the light feels too warm for your hand, then it’s going to be too much light for your cannabis.

**LEDs** generate very little heat, but can still light-stress plants, so it’s best to keep LEDs the distance recommended by the manufacturer. Each grow light is different, so it’s hard to make generalizations, but just about every LED needs to be at least 10″ inches away from your plants. In my experience, most hobby-sized LEDs (100-300watt true power draw) need to be kept 18-24″ away in my experience. Some LEDs need to be kept even further away, up to 3 feet or more for some of the newer, [extremely powerful LED model](https://www.amazon.com/Horticulture-Lighting-Group-Quantum-Dimmable/dp/B07R5C4CKR/ref=as_li_ss_tl?dchild=1&keywords=hlg+300&qid=1592712197&s=lawn-garden&sr=1-3&th=1&linkCode=ll1&tag=4200a7-20&linkId=0661485f29c15637d8be2ee4ba2d080c&language=en_US)s.
LED grow lights usually need to be kept at least 18-24″ away to prevent light burn
[](https://www.growweedeasy.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/05/hlg-quantum-board-led-grow-light-flowering-stage.jpg)
Use caution when attempting to move your grow lights closer, and avoid moving them more than an inch closer in a day, so you can watch the plant’s reaction.
**Note: These brown spots are the sign of a nutrient deficiency** (mainly a [calcium deficiency](https://www.growweedeasy.com/cannabis-plant-problems/calcium-deficiency)), _not_ light burn. They’re usually caused by incorrect pH at the roots. They are often confused with light stress because they appear first on the leaves closest to the light, but that’s only because those leaves work the hardest. Unless you fix the underlying deficiency, the symptoms will keep spreading no matter how far you keep your grow lights from the plants.
[](https://www.growweedeasy.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/not-light-stress-these-cannabis-symptoms-brown-burnt-spots-calcium-deficiency-caused-by-low-ph.jpg)
**Make sure your grow space is light-proof!** (Unless growing an [autoflowering strain](https://www.growweedeasy.com/why-beginners-should-grow-auto-flowering-strains), then it doesn’t matter)
If you’re growing photoperiod cannabis plants, you need to make sure no light can get into your growing area when the grow lights are turned off. These types of plants need full darkness during their “night” (dark phase) or they won’t flower. If they start receiving light during their dark period while they’re budding, it can cause them to “[herm](https://www.growweedeasy.com/cannabis-plant-problems/male-plants-hermies-bananas)” (which makes buds seedy) or “[re-vegetate](https://www.growweedeasy.com/cannabis-plant-problems/accidental-re-vegging)” (where the buds die off and the plant reverts back to the vegetative stage).
If you’re growing a photoperiod plant, light leaks can cause [seedy buds](https://www.growweedeasy.com/cannabis-plant-problems/male-plants-hermies-bananas).
[](https://www.growweedeasy.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/10/seedy-bud-calyxes-popping-open.jpg)
Or [re-vegging](https://www.growweedeasy.com/cannabis-plant-problems/accidental-re-vegging) (the main symptoms are rounded leaves on long stems and dying buds).
## [](https://www.growweedeasy.com/wp-content/uploads/2015/12/unhappy-reveg.jpg)
##  Step 5: Diagnose Pests (sometimes you never actually see the bugs)
**Uncover the Invisible Enemy: Is it Actually a Cannabis Pest?**
When growing in soil, bugs are a common pest in marijuana gardens. At GrowWeedEasy.com, we believe that [growing marijuana in coco](https://www.growweedeasy.com/coco-coir) is often simpler for beginners because it automatically eliminates many of the bug problems growers run into when growing in soil while achieving faster growth.
Sometimes you don’t see bugs. [Thrips are an example of a pest](https://www.growweedeasy.com/cannabis-plant-problems/thrips) that leaves markings on the leaves which can be mistaken for deficiencies, yet growers may not see the bugs themselves.
[](https://www.growweedeasy.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/08/thrips-leaf-damage-jennybee698.jpg)
[Broad mites](https://www.growweedeasy.com/cannabis-plant-problems/broad-mites) are also commonly mistaken for other issues because the bugs are usually too small to see even if you know what to look for. Broad mites cause twisted growth and shiny, discolored cannabis leaves.
[](https://www.growweedeasy.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/08/marijuana-broad-mite-damage-blistered-leaves-twisted-growth.jpg)
[Hemp russet mites](https://www.growweedeasy.com/cannabis-plant-problems/hemp-russet-mites) “poop” out extra stuff at the top of the plant that can be mistaken for mold
[](https://www.growweedeasy.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/09/hemp-russet-mites-yellow-mass-on-cannabis.jpg)
Usually, you can tell if plants have bugs because you’ll either see the bugs themselves, or the leaves will look like they’ve been bitten (with spots or holes in the leaves).
_What to Do:_ If you notice _any_ signs of bugs or mold, _don’t wait._ Act quickly!
A major pest for cannabis growers is the dreaded [spider mite](https://www.growweedeasy.com/cannabis-plant-problems/how-to-get-rid-of-spider-mites). Spider mites often sneak in on cannabis clones from another grower, or from another grow room. They’ve been nicknamed “the borg” because they seem to resurrect from the dead and can overrun an entire grow room or ruin a whole crop. Thankfully, when spider mites come from outside plants, like your rose bushes, they’re not specialists in cannabis and are often easier to get rid of. Starting from seeds is a good way to help prevent a tough pest infestation.
[Spider mites](https://www.growweedeasy.com/cannabis-plant-problems/how-to-get-rid-of-spider-mites) are another common marijuana pest. You can often see them moving under the leaves, or you’ll notice the webbing and bite marks.
[](https://www.growweedeasy.com/wp-content/uploads/2013/07/red-spider-mites.jpg)
[Fungus gnats](https://www.growweedeasy.com/cannabis-plant-problems/fungus-gnats) are another common cannabis pest. They look like tiny flies buzzing around the soil and are the result of overwatering.
[](https://www.growweedeasy.com/wp-content/uploads/2014/06/fungus-gnat-quarter.jpg)
[Leafhoppers](https://www.growweedeasy.com/cannabis-plant-problems/leafhoppers) look pretty, but they munch on your plants and can spread diseases like [beet curly top virus](https://www.growweedeasy.com/cannabis-plant-problems/beet-curly-top-virus) so don’t ignore them!
[](https://www.growweedeasy.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/08/picture-of-leafhopper-bug-on-marijuana-leaf.jpg)
When you believe you have bugs…
  * The first step is to [identify what type of bugs or pests](https://www.growweedeasy.com/bugs-pests-symptoms-marijuana-grow) you actually have
  * The next step is treatment. [Get a list of safe cannabis insecticides](https://www.growweedeasy.com/safe-cannabis-pesticides).

As mentioned earlier, good air circulation and a slight breeze are not only good for the plant by itself, but will also disturb bugs and make it harder for them to turn your plant into a home.
